topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the worn spine of 'Whispers in the Gloom,' the book that had been the bedrock of his literary world for years. He’d devoured every sentence, memorized every cadence. Then, a footnote in a recent literary critique caught his eye—a jarringly similar narrative arc, a near-identical description of a pivotal character's despair, mirrored in a work by the celebrated author, Silas Vance. A cold knot tightened in Elias’s stomach, a physical clenching that made his jaw ache. He reread the passage, his breath catching in his throat. It wasn't just similarity; it was an echo, a theft of his very voice. He spent the next day staring blankly at his own manuscript, the words feeling suddenly hollow, as if someone had lifted his scaffolding and replaced it with cheap plaster. Every time he tried to write, a phantom feeling of exposure washed over him, leaving him physically shaky, his hands trembling slightly as he gripped his pen.

[story 2]

“It’s uncanny, isn’t it?” Clara murmured, swirling the lukewarm tea in her mug. She had just discovered that the intricate, emotionally resonant plot of her debut novel, the one she poured her soul into, was suspiciously close to a recent, highly publicized work by the reigning master, Julian Thorne. The resemblance wasn't just thematic; it was structural, the subtle turns of phrase, the unexpected emotional pivots—all there, meticulously copied. Clara felt a distinct pressure behind her eyes, a dull ache that made focusing on anything else impossible. She kept glancing over her shoulder, half-expecting someone to be watching her, cataloging her every move. When she finally looked back at her laptop screen, the words seemed alien, borrowed, and the silence of her apartment felt suddenly vast and suffocating. She pushed the chair back abruptly, the scrape of wood on the floor sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet room.

[story 3]

Marcus sat rigidly in his armchair, the heavy volume resting on his lap feeling suddenly like a lead weight. He had been anticipating the review of his latest collection, a moment of pure, unadulterated validation. Instead, he found himself consumed by a gnawing suspicion. The narrative structure of his central character’s journey—the very core of his artistic vision—was strikingly parallel to a recent, bestselling novel by Anya Volkov. A wave of nausea rolled through him, forcing him to grip the armrests until his knuckles turned white. He kept running his thumb over the cover of his book, a frantic, repetitive motion. Every time he tried to articulate his own ideas, the words felt clumsy, inadequate, as if the genuine spark had been extinguished, replaced by a cold, external imitation. He stood up, pacing the narrow confines of his study, the floorboards groaning under his agitated steps.
